original_abstract:
This article examines the intensifying legal restrictions on the immigration of elderly family members—primarily parents and grandparents—in major Western destination states. While immigration policies have historically favored younger, "economically productive" individuals, the right of settled immigrants to reunite with their elderly relatives was long considered an unquestioned component of family-based migration. However, the author argues that over the last few decades, traditional and modern settler states (such as Canada, Australia, and New Zealand), as well as liberal non-immigrant regimes like the United Kingdom, have engaged in a systematic but largely unremarked assault on this class of migrants.

The study categorizes these restrictions into a "toolkit" of exclusionary measures, including prohibitive income requirements for sponsors, drastically reduced quotas that result in decades-long waitlists, and the shift from a "medical model" of health inadmissibility (focused on infectious disease) to an "economic model" (focused on potential healthcare costs). Furthermore, the article analyzes how states are reframing elderly relatives as "tourists" or "investors" rather than family members with a right to cohabitation, effectively making reunification a privilege reserved for the wealthy.

Through a critical review of policy discourse and judicial decisions, the author finds that these restrictions are justified by a narrow, often flawed economic rationalism that ignores the social and indirect economic contributions of elderly family members. Despite challenges based on constitutional and international human rights law, courts have remained deferential to executive authority. The article concludes that these increasingly draconian measures are not only unjust and inhumane but also legally and politically unsupported, calling for a reversal of policies that treat the elderly as mere fiscal burdens.
humanized_abstract:
Older migrants are increasingly being subjected to stricter regulations in relation to family reunification concerning the immigration of their elderly parents and grandparents, in particular in major Western destination countries. While the rules of family reunification generally tend to privilege younger, economically productive immigrants, the reunion of settled immigrants with their elderly relatives has generally been considered an undisputed right within the framework of family reunification for immigrant families. The article contests this general assumption and shows that over the last few decades, both traditional and modern settler countries such as Canada, Australia and New Zealand, as well as liberal non-immigration countries such as the UK, have all, more or less covertly, engaged in a process of marginalising older migrants as a category of immigrants.

According to the study, restrictions imposed on families who wish to reunite are set out in a "toolkit" of exclusion measures that include the imposition of minimum income threshold for official sponsorships, drastically reducing family reunification quotas so that they effectively amount to a decades-long waiting list; and a switch from a "medical model" (based on control of infectious diseases) to an "economic model" (where the objective is to ensure that medical expenses are covered). In addition, states are increasingly portraying elderly family members as "tourists" or "investors" as opposed to simply being members of a family entitled to live with their loved ones. In this way the right to join one's family is turned into a privilege for those who can afford it.

This article explores recent policy developments and judicial decisions on elderly caregivers in Singapore through the prism of constitutional justice, arguing that recent policy developments and judicial decisions which restrict such caregivers constitute a grave injustice to caregivers and older care-recipients alike. Rationally argued within the economics framework which underpins this policy, it argues that this restriction is based on a limited and arguably tenuous economic argument which has been unchallenged and thus taken as true in policy circles and as a result in judicial decision-making, and hence ignored is the significant social and often unseen economic benefits such caregivers bring to society and to care-recipients. This argument challenges the justification for such policy restrictions while arguing that the challenge based on constitutional and international human rights law has been weak. In sum, recent policy developments and their judicial endorsement that amount to what may arguably be described as the harshest persecution of the elderly and elderly caregivers the world has seen, in the name of saving the state money by forcing families to hire caregivers in a fully-marketised employment relations regime, is argued here to be based on flawed reasoning and that it is imperative therefore that such policies be reviewed with a view to having them reversed in their current form.
